scenes.title: Conditional Probability & Bayes' Rule
scenes.description: Build intuition from events and areas to conditional probability and Bayes' rule — with three real-world examples.
scenes.markdown:
# Conditional Probability & Bayes' Rule

## The Unit Square

Represent the complete sample space $\Omega$ as a **unit square** (area = 1). Any event occupies a fraction of that area equal to its probability:

$$P(\text{event}) = \text{fraction of square covered}$$

## Conditional Probability

Conditioning on event $B$ means **restricting the sample space** to only the outcomes where $B$ occurs. The probability of $A$ within that restricted space:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Geometrically: **zoom into the B-region**, then ask what fraction of it contains $A$.

## The Asymmetry

$P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$ in general — same intersection, different denominators:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \qquad P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

## Bayes' Rule

From the symmetry of the intersection $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$:

$$\boxed{P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}}$$

**Belief update interpretation:**
- $P(A)$ — **prior**: belief before observing $B$
- $P(B \mid A)$ — **likelihood**: how probable is $B$ if $A$ is true?
- $P(B)$ — **marginal**: how probable is $B$ overall? (normalizing constant)
- $P(A \mid B)$ — **posterior**: updated belief after observing $B$

## Why It Surprises Everyone

Three examples where intuition fails due to **base rate neglect** — forgetting how rare the condition is:

| Scenario | Naive guess | Actual answer |
|----------|------------|---------------|
| Medical test (0.1% disease, 99% accurate) | ~99% | **~4.7%** |
| Spam filter ("Nigeria" keyword) | high | **~73.8%** |
| Tornado radar (2% base rate, 95% detection) | ~95% | **~39.3%** |

## Key Formulas

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{A})}$$

steps.title: The Sample Space
steps.description:
Every possible outcome lives inside this square. **Area = probability** — the entire square has area 1.0 (100%). An event covering half the square has probability 0.5.

All of probability theory lives here. Use the sliders to set $P(A)$ and $P(B)$ — we'll use them in the next steps.

steps.title: Events A and B
steps.description:
**Event A** (blue) spans the left $P(A)$ fraction — a vertical strip. **Event B** (orange) spans the bottom $P(B)$ fraction — a horizontal strip.

The two strips overlap in the bottom-left corner. That overlap is $A \cap B$ — outcomes where **both** A and B occur.

**Independence check**: if knowing B tells you nothing about A, then $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ — the blue fraction within the orange strip equals the total blue fraction.

info.content:
### Events A and B
$P(A) = {{pA}}$ (blue strip width)
$P(B) = {{pB}}$ (orange strip height)
---
$P(\overline{A}) = {{toFixed(1-pA,2)}}$
$P(\overline{B}) = {{toFixed(1-pB,2)}}$
---
If independent:
$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$
$\quad\quad\quad\; = {{toFixed(pA*pB,3)}}$

steps.title: The Intersection and $P(A \mid B)$
steps.description:
The **green region** is $A \cap B$ — outcomes where **both** A and B occur. It lives entirely inside the B strip.

$P(A \mid B)$ is the **fraction of the B strip that is green**. Adjust the slider below to change this directly. When $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$, the events are **independent** — knowing B happened doesn't change how likely A is.

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

sliders.label: $P(A \mid B)$ — fraction of B containing A

info.content:
### Intersection & Conditional
$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$
$\quad\quad\quad\; = {{pAgivenB}} \times {{pB}}$
$\quad\quad\quad\; = {{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB,3)}}$
---
$P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pAgivenB,3)}}$
$P(A) = {{pA}}$
---
**Independent?**
{{abs(pAgivenB - pA) < 0.02 ? 'Yes — P(A|B) ≈ P(A)' : pAgivenB > pA ? 'No — B makes A more likely' : 'No — B makes A less likely'}}

steps.title: Conditioning: Zoom into B
steps.description:
**Conditioning on B** shrinks the sample space to only the B-region. We literally **zoom in** on it.

The **elevated square** (z = 2.5) shows the view after conditioning on B: the B strip stretched to fill the entire square. Within it, the green region is $A$ — its width is exactly $P(A \mid B)$.

The dashed connecting lines trace the geometric mapping: the B strip expands to become the new sample space.

Use the **Side** camera view to see the elevation clearly.

info.content:
### Zoom into B
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$
---
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB,3)}}$
$P(B) = {{pB}}$
---
$P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pAgivenB,3)}}$
---
Zoomed view (top):
Green fraction = ${{toFixed(pAgivenB*10,1)}}$ / $10$
$\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\; = {{toFixed(pAgivenB,3)}}$ ✓

steps.title: Asymmetry: $P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$
steps.description:
Both conditionals use the **same green intersection** — but divide by different things:

- $P(A \mid B)$ = what fraction of the **B strip** (orange) is green?
- $P(B \mid A)$ = what fraction of the **A strip** (blue dashed) is green?

The blue dashed frame highlights the A strip. The green region within it is $A \cap B$ — its height divided by the A strip height gives $P(B \mid A)$.

They're equal only when $P(A) = P(B)$. This asymmetry is the source of countless reasoning errors.

info.content:
### Asymmetry
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB,3)}}$
---
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{{{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB,3)}}}{{{pB}}} = {{toFixed(pAgivenB,3)}}$

$P(B \mid A) = \dfrac{{{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB,3)}}}{{{pA}}} = {{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB/pA,3)}}$
---
{{abs(pAgivenB - pAgivenB*pB/pA) < 0.01 ? 'Equal here: P(A) ≈ P(B)' : pAgivenB > pAgivenB*pB/pA ? 'P(A|B) > P(B|A)' : 'P(A|B) < P(B|A)'}}

steps.title: Bayes' Rule
steps.description:
The intersection is symmetric:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

Rearranging gives **Bayes' rule** — the formula for updating beliefs with evidence:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

- **Prior** $P(A)$: what you believed before seeing B
- **Likelihood** $P(B \mid A)$: how probable is B if A is true?
- **Marginal** $P(B)$: how probable is B overall?
- **Posterior** $P(A \mid B)$: updated belief after seeing B

The three examples next show where naive intuition fails spectacularly.

info.content:
### Bayes' Rule
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$
---
**Prior** $P(A) = {{pA}}$
**Likelihood** $P(B \mid A) = {{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB/pA,3)}}$
**Marginal** $P(B) = {{pB}}$
---
**Posterior** $P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pAgivenB,3)}}$
---
Verify:
$\dfrac{{{toFixed(pAgivenB*pB/pA,3)}} \times {{pA}}}{{{pB}}} = {{toFixed(pAgivenB,3)}}$ ✓

steps.title: Example 1 — Medical Testing
steps.description:
A disease affects **0.1%** of the population. A test is **99% sensitive** (correctly detects disease) and has a **2% false positive rate** (positive result despite no disease).

**You test positive. What's the probability you actually have the disease?**

Most people say ~99%. The real answer is ~**4.7%**.

**Left column** (red) = people WITH disease (width = prevalence). **Right column** (blue) = without disease. **Bottom strip** of each column = those who test positive. The posterior $P(D \mid +)$ is the bright green area divided by the full bottom strip.

steps.title: Example 2 — Spam Filtering
steps.description:
**15%** of emails are spam. **80%** of spam emails contain "Nigeria". Only **5%** of legitimate emails contain it.

**An email arrives with "Nigeria" in the subject — is it spam?**

Left column (orange) = spam emails. Right column (blue) = legitimate. Bottom strip of each = emails containing the keyword. The posterior $P(\text{spam} \mid \text{word})$ is the orange bottom area divided by the total bottom area.

Try adding a second word: if $P(\text{word}_2 \mid \text{spam})$ is also high but $P(\text{word}_2 \mid \text{legit})$ is tiny, the posterior jumps toward 99%.

info.content:
### Spam Filter
Keyword: "Nigeria"
---
$P(\text{spam}) = {{pSpam}}$
$P(W \mid \text{spam}) = {{pWspam}}$
$P(W \mid \text{legit}) = {{pWleg}}$
---
**Per 10,000 emails:**
Spam with word: {{toFixed(pSpam*pWspam*10000,0)}}
Legit with word: {{toFixed((1-pSpam)*pWleg*10000,0)}}
Total with word: {{toFixed((pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg)*10000,0)}}
---
$P(\text{spam} \mid W) = {{toFixed(pSpam*pWspam/(pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg)*100,1)}}\%$

steps.title: Example 3 — Tornado Warning
steps.description:
Only **2%** of severe storm alerts involve real tornadoes. Radar detects **95%** of actual tornadoes (true detection rate). But it triggers for **3%** of non-tornado storms (false alarm rate).

**The radar fires. What's the probability of an actual tornado?**

Even with a 95%-accurate sensor, most alerts are false alarms — because tornadoes are so rare. The answer is ~**39%**.

Left column (red) = real tornadoes. Right column (gray) = non-tornado storms. Bottom strip = radar signal fired.

**Try the base rate slider**: dragging prevalence up dramatically increases the posterior — rarity is what kills the accuracy.

scenes.prompt:
You are teaching conditional probability and Bayes' rule using the unit square area model.

## Core Geometric Intuition
- The 10×10 square represents the full sample space (area = 1)
- Blue A strip: vertical, width = P(A)
- Orange B strip: horizontal, height = P(B)
- Green intersection A∩B: within B strip, width = P(A|B)
- Step 4 shows the 3D 'zoom into B' — the B strip elevated and stretched to full size at z=2.5

## Slider Connections to Always Make
When sliders change, explicitly connect to geometry:
- P(A) → blue strip width changes
- P(B) → orange strip height changes; posterior changes even if likelihoods are fixed (base rate effect!)
- P(A|B) → green width within B strip changes

## For Real-World Examples (steps 7–9)
- Base rate neglect is the key insight: rare condition → most positives are false
- Medical: at 0.1% prevalence, even a 99% test gives only ~4.7% posterior — false positives outnumber true positives 20:1
- Spam: more intuitive, shows how keyword evidence shifts belief; mention Naive Bayes classifiers
- Tornado: connect to policy decisions — what posterior threshold justifies issuing a warning?

## Exploration Prompts to Offer Proactively
- 'Try setting disease prevalence to 1% vs 0.01% and compare the posteriors'
- 'What sensitivity would make the medical test truly useful at 0.1% prevalence?'
- 'For the tornado warning, what base rate makes the posterior cross 50%?'
- 'Can you derive what P(B|A) is from the sliders on step 5?'

## Follow-Up Topics to Suggest
- Sequential updates: applying Bayes twice with two pieces of evidence
- The prosecutor's fallacy: confusing P(evidence|innocent) with P(innocent|evidence)
- Likelihood ratios as a cleaner framework: LR = P(E|H) / P(E|¬H)
- Naive Bayes classifiers in machine learning
